items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom
items.title: Kioxia erases 51% of its value in a month as Japan's AI darling runs out of buyers
items.idea: Kioxia fell as much as 14% on Friday, down 51% from its mid-June peak, wiping roughly ¥29.5 trillion in market value. A month ago it was briefly Japan's most valuable company after a 600%+ YTD run; it has now slid to fourth place. Bain Capital's exit added to the unease.
items.angle: This is the cleanest single data point that Asia's AI-equity euphoria is unwinding via forced-selling mechanics, not fundamentals — pairs directly with the Korea margin-call and leveraged-ETF items below.
items.tweetA: Kioxia was Japan's most valuable company a month ago. Today it's down 51% from its June peak, ¥29.5T erased. The company didn't change — the willingness to pay any price for AI exposure did.
items.tweetB: 600% up, then -51% in a month. Kioxia is what happens when a stock becomes the local proxy for a global thesis and leverage does the rest. Worth asking which other AI names are quietly funded by borrowed conviction.

charts.desc: No embedded chart image, but the article's price chart of the 600%-then-51% round trip is a clean tweetable shape. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: 1 in 30 working-age South Koreans just got a margin call
items.idea: 1.2 million Korean investors have been hit with margin calls in the last week — roughly 1 in 30 working-age adults in the country. QQQ is testing a weekly close below $700, which would complete an 8+ week topping pattern and project another 15-20% of downside for US tech.
items.angle: Korea is ground zero for AI-trade leverage unwinding into the real economy, not just markets — a specific, dated technical level (QQQ $700 weekly close) gives this a trigger to watch.
items.tweetA: 1 in 30 working-age South Koreans got a margin call this week. That's not a sector correction, that's a household balance-sheet event.
items.tweetB: QQQ closing below $700 on a weekly basis completes an 8-week top and projects 15-20% more downside on the charts. Nobody's pricing that into buy-the-AI-dip yet.

charts.desc: Article strings together ~12 daily charts (IBM, Micron, Sandisk, CAT, Cameco, NexGen, SpaceX, QQQ, TSX-V, Valero, Deere, Hudbay) — the QQQ topping-pattern chart alone tells the story.

items.title: SpaceX closes below its IPO price for the first time ever — 1.4B shares unlock by Christmas
items.idea: SpaceX's share price closed below its IPO price for the first time ever on Thursday, with 1.4 billion shares set to unlock by Christmas. Separately, gold and silver miners are trading as if priced for another $400-500/oz of downside even as recent CPI/PPI prints have surprised soft, i.e. deflation, not inflation.
items.angle: The SpaceX lockup gives a dated, mechanical catalyst for further private-market AI marks to compress; the gold-miner pricing is a live contradiction of the debase-everything trade everyone assumes is in force.
items.tweetA: SpaceX just closed below its IPO price for the first time ever. 1.4 billion shares unlock by Christmas. The first-trillionaire math was always one lockup away from this.
items.tweetB: Gold miners are priced for another $400-500/oz of downside even with gold near records. Either the miners are wrong, or gold is about to have a rough quarter — and the CPI/PPI prints this month lean toward the miners.

charts.desc: SpaceX daily chart and gold-miner charts referenced in the piece.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom
items.title: JPMorgan: the leveraged-ETF deleveraging cycle has three more months to run
items.idea: A 3x leveraged ETF that loses 30% on a 10% index decline needs the index to rally 11.1% just to get back to even — and even then the ETF is still ~7% below where it started, due to volatility decay. Leveraged equity ETF assets are down ~13% since the June peak; leveraged memory-chip products are down 34%. JPMorgan's Panigirtzoglou expects the deleveraging cycle (ETFs, options, margin accounts) to run roughly three more months.
items.angle: This decay math explains why leveraged AI-chip products can't recover even if the underlying stabilizes — it's a structural, dated overhang (~3 months), not a sentiment problem that can flip on one good print.
items.tweetA: A 3x leveraged ETF that drops 30% needs the index to rally 11.1% just to break even — and even then it's still down ~7%. That volatility-decay math is why leveraged semiconductor ETFs are down 34% since June while the underlying chip complex is down ~22%.
items.tweetB: JPMorgan says the deleveraging cycle has three more months to run. If that's right, every AI-dip buy between now and October is fighting a structural seller, not a sentiment problem.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the leveraged-ETF asset-decline series.

items.title: Investor sentiment hasn't capitulated even though chip stocks already have
items.idea: Investors Intelligence's Bull/Bear Ratio rose to 3.12 last week, well above its historical average and approaching excessively bullish territory — even as the AI/semiconductor complex sits roughly 22% below its June highs.
items.angle: Sentiment (still bullish) and price (already down 22% in chips) are diverging hard. That mismatch is usually resolved by more selling, not a bottom.
items.tweetA: Bull/Bear ratio at 3.12 — near excessively-bullish territory — while chip stocks sit 22% below their June highs. Sentiment hasn't capitulated. That's not how bottoms usually form.
items.tweetB: Everyone's calling this an AI correction. The Investors Intelligence survey says otherwise: investors are still net-bullish at levels that have historically preceded more pain, not less.

charts.desc: Bull/Bear Ratio chart via Yardeni Research — a clean, single-line sentiment chart worth saving.

items.title: Iran's currency is collapsing faster than oil prices are moving
items.idea: Robin Brooks notes the US blockade is disabling empty Iranian oil tankers used as floating storage and taking out oil infrastructure. The Iranian Rial is falling to new lows even though headline oil prices haven't spiked dramatically.
items.angle: A collapsing currency alongside contained oil prices is the tell that the blockade is degrading Iran's economy faster than it's constraining global supply — a leading indicator ahead of the Bab el-Mandeb escalation risk below.
items.tweetA: The Iranian Rial is making new lows while oil hasn't spiked much. That's the tell the blockade is actually working — it's hitting Iran's economy faster than it's hitting global oil supply.
items.tweetB: Floating-storage tankers getting disabled plus a collapsing Rial. Iran's decade-old sanctions-evasion playbook is being dismantled in real time, and the oil market barely noticed.

charts.desc: Rial FX chart via @robin_j_brooks.

items.title: Goldman's High Beta Momentum basket is having its worst month in 17 years
items.idea: Goldman's High Beta Momentum basket is on track for its worst monthly performance in roughly 17 years.
items.angle: A 17-year record for a momentum factor confirms this week's selling is a factor-wide unwind, not a chip-sector story — corroborates the JPMorgan leveraged-ETF and Korea margin-call items above from an entirely different desk.
items.tweetA: Goldman's High Beta Momentum basket is having its worst month in 17 years. Three different desks are describing the same mechanical unwind from three different angles this week — when the plumbing all points the same way, listen to the plumbing.
items.tweetB: 17 years is a long look-back for a momentum basket to have its worst month. Last time it was this bad, the vintage alone tells you this isn't about semiconductors.

charts.desc: Goldman High Beta Momentum performance chart via Bloomberg.

items.title: Cyclical leadership is still intact under the surface, even as headline indices wobble
items.idea: Despite day-to-day rotation, the spread between cyclical and defensive industry performance is still positive — a condition that has historically coincided with above-average S&P 500 forward returns.
items.angle: Directly contradicts the doom-and-gloom framing dominating this digest — a breadth/leadership signal says risk-on under the hood, echoing the Norseman breadth thesis further down.
items.tweetA: Cyclicals are still outperforming defensives under the hood, even with headline indices wobbling. That spread has historically preceded above-average S&P returns. The tape is arguing with the mood.
items.tweetB: Two signals landed in the same inbox this week: chip stocks in a bear market, and cyclical leadership still intact. Pick your lens and you get a completely different market.

charts.desc: Cyclical-vs-defensive spread chart via @tpmrsignals.

items.title: Iran asks the Houthis to prep a second chokepoint blockade — Bab el-Mandeb, not just Hormuz
items.idea: Iranian officials asked Yemen's Houthis to prepare to block the Red Sea gateway if the US strikes Iranian energy infrastructure. Bab el-Mandeb is only 18 miles wide, funneled into two 2-mile channels, and carried 8.8 million barrels/day of oil and products plus 12% of global trade and 30% of container traffic. Trump has threatened Iranian bridges and power plants, with reports placing the strike decision window as early as next week.
items.angle: This converts a single-chokepoint Hormuz scenario into a two-front maritime squeeze conditional on one specific, dated US decision — the market is pricing a Hormuz premium but not yet a Houthi-activated Red Sea premium on top of it.
items.tweetA: Hormuz gets all the headlines. Bab el-Mandeb carries 8.8mbpd and 30% of global container traffic through a channel two miles wide. Iran just asked the Houthis to be ready to shut it.
items.tweetB: The market is pricing a Hormuz premium. It is not pricing a Houthi-activated Red Sea premium on top of it — and per BBC, Trump's decision window on Iranian energy sites is as early as next week.

charts.desc: Text-only policy brief, no embedded chart. Worth opening for the full sourcing (Reuters, EIA, UNCTAD, Foreign Policy).

items.title: Stripe + Advent offer $53.4B for PayPal — largest fintech acquisition ever
items.idea: Stripe, alongside private equity firm Advent International, has reportedly offered $60.50/share — a $53.4 billion deal — for PayPal. PayPal's board is expected to meet on the offer as early as next week. If completed, it would be the largest fintech acquisition ever.
items.angle: This is the kinetic version of the stablecoin land-grab story below — PayPal gives Stripe distribution to route volume through its own Tempo stablecoin rail.
items.tweetA: $53.4B for PayPal would make this the largest fintech acquisition ever. Stripe isn't buying a checkout button, it's buying roughly 400 million wallets to plug into its Tempo stablecoin rail.
items.tweetB: Two newsletters framed the same deal two different ways in one day: TheStreet called it a takeover bid, Bankless called it the great customer race for stablecoins. Same $53.4B, and both readings are right.

charts.desc: No chart — text news item.

items.title: IBM's worst day since 1968
items.idea: IBM posted its worst single trading day since 1968, a move framed as handing a gift to Micron in the memory/AI trade.
items.angle: A 58-year record for IBM landed in the same week the memory/AI complex is officially in a bear market — a standalone dislocation worth flagging separately from the Korea/Kioxia leverage unwind.
items.tweetA: IBM's worst single day since 1968. Fifty-eight years of trading history, and this week broke the record. Whatever happened in that print, it clearly was not priced in.
items.tweetB: IBM's worst-day-since-1968 landed the same week Micron blew out earnings and still got sold. The market is rewriting which AI-adjacent names get the benefit of the doubt in real time.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: SK Hynix pops on its Nasdaq debut the same week Korean holders get margin-called on the same stock
items.idea: SK Hynix made a jaw-dropping gain on its Nasdaq trading debut this week, even as the broader Korean memory complex (including SK Hynix's home listing) is reportedly under severe margin-call pressure domestically, per the Liquidation piece above.
items.angle: A cross-listing divergence: US investors are bidding up the new listing while domestic Korean holders are forced sellers of the same underlying business — a liquidity-arbitrage setup worth watching for a squeeze or a fade.
items.tweetA: SK Hynix popped on its Nasdaq debut the same week 1.2 million Korean retail accounts got margin-called on the same stock at home. Two markets, two completely different reads on the same memory maker.
items.tweetB: When a stock is a forced seller at home and a hot new listing abroad simultaneously, that's not conviction, that's a liquidity arbitrage waiting to close.

charts.desc: No chart in email; likely price chart in article.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom
items.title: TSMC beat on every metric and raised every forward number — and the sector still fell
items.idea: TSMC reported net income up 77% YoY to T$706.6 billion (~$22 billion), gross margin of 67.7%, raised its 2026 revenue-growth guide to slightly above 40%, and lifted capex guidance to $60-64 billion. ASML delivered a strong quarter and raised its outlook; Micron posted a blowout result. Yet the semiconductor/memory sector is still roughly 22% below its mid-June high, firmly in bear-market territory.
items.angle: Peak priced-for-perfection: a company beating on every line and raising every forward number still can't hold its stock, because the bar embedded in the price was higher than the guide.
items.tweetA: TSMC just posted 77% YoY net income growth, 67.7% gross margins, and raised both its revenue-growth guide (>40%) and capex guide ($60-64B) — and chip stocks are still down 22% from June highs. When the beat isn't big enough, the trade stopped being about the beat.
items.tweetB: TSMC's capex guide just moved to $60-64B for 2026. That's not a company pulling back on AI, that's a company printing record profit and still spending faster than the market wants to reward it for.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the sector-vs-earnings divergence chart.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom
items.title: KOSPI realized volatility just hit levels rarely seen outside major historical disruptions
items.idea: Realized volatility in the KOSPI has surged toward levels rarely seen outside major historical disruptions, with foreign investors selling Korean equities at the fastest pace in decades, amplified by leveraged and derivative-based products.
items.angle: Quantifies exactly how extreme the Korea unwind is beyond the margin-call headline count — a volatility-regime data point, not just a sentiment one.
items.tweetA: KOSPI realized volatility just hit levels rarely seen outside major historical disruptions, and foreign investors are selling at the fastest pace in decades. This isn't AI profit-taking, it's a volatility event.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the Nasdaq drawdown percentage. The number that actually matters this week is KOSPI realized vol — it tells you how mechanical and forced the selling really is.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the KOSPI realized-vol series.

items.title: A model-delay headline, not a spending headline, broadened Thursday's growth selloff
items.idea: Growth-stock weakness broadened later in Thursday's session after reports that Alphabet's Gemini 3.5 Pro model will be delayed.
items.angle: Every prior AI drawdown this cycle has been about capex fears. This is the first time a specific product-delivery slip moved the tape same-day — worth watching whether delivery risk becomes its own priced factor.
items.tweetA: A single headline — Gemini 3.5 Pro delayed — broadened Thursday's growth selloff. All year the worry has been the AI trade breaks on spending. The first real crack this week came from a shipping delay instead.
items.tweetB: Every prior AI drawdown was about capex fear. This one has a new ingredient: a specific model delay moving the tape same-day. Watch whether delivery risk becomes its own factor.

charts.desc: No chart — text market-recap item.

items.title: Nasdaq -1.5%, equal-weight S&P +1.0% — same day
items.idea: On Thursday the Nasdaq fell 1.5% and the S&P 500 fell 0.5%, yet 8 of 11 S&P sectors finished green and the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index gained +1.0%.
items.angle: Headline index moves this week are being driven almost entirely by a handful of mega-cap AI names — the median stock actually had a good day, a materially cleaner read than the cap-weighted print.
items.tweetA: Nasdaq -1.5%, but 8 of 11 S&P sectors were green and the equal-weight S&P gained +1.0% the same day. This week's market selloff is really a mega-cap AI selloff wearing an index-wide costume.
items.tweetB: If you only watch cap-weighted indices, this week looks scary. If you watch the equal-weight S&P, it looks like a healthy rotation. Which one is lying to you depends entirely on what you own.

charts.desc: Full breakdown promised inside (SPX/Nasdaq/Russell technicals) — worth opening.

items.title: The AI interconnect bottleneck might have a boring, 25-year-old fix: GaAs VCSELs
items.idea: PicoJool CEO Al Yuen argues GaAs VCSEL-based optics are the unconstrained-supply answer for scale-up interconnects, versus substrate-constrained indium phosphide (InP) single-mode solutions. The company just announced a 200-gigabit-per-lane device with a roadmap to 3.2T and 12.8T via bi-directional links and 2D arrays, built on a fabless model using WIN Semiconductors capacity.
items.angle: A component-level de-risking thesis inside the optics-supply-chain story: if GaAs VCSELs really are unconstrained where InP isn't, that changes which names carry supply-chain risk in the AI-networking buildout.
items.tweetA: The AI interconnect bottleneck story has mostly been about InP substrate supply. PicoJool's pitch: GaAs VCSELs are unconstrained and can scale to 12.8T via 2D arrays — a quietly different answer to the same capacity problem everyone's worried about.
items.tweetB: 25-year-old VCSEL technology is now being pitched as the pragmatic fix for AI's optical interconnect crunch, instead of the newer co-packaged-optics solutions everyone's chasing. Sometimes the boring supply chain wins.

charts.desc: Podcast transcript, no charts — technical roadmap detail (8×200/16×100/32×50 flavors) worth opening.

items.title: Homebuilder sentiment has now been below 40 for the longest stretch since 2012
items.idea: The NAHB Housing Market Index fell 2 points to 34 in July, matching April as the lowest since September's near-4-year low of 32, and has sat below the 50 poor/good threshold for 27 consecutive months and below 40 for 15 consecutive months — the longest such stretch since 2012. 37% of builders cut prices in July (up from 35% in June, 32% in May); sales-incentive usage hit 63%, the 16th straight month at 60%+.
items.angle: A clean, monotonic three-month deterioration series (32%→35%→37% of builders cutting prices) that's getting almost no attention this week because chip-stock volatility is dominating headlines.
items.tweetA: NAHB builder sentiment has been below 40 for 15 straight months, the longest stretch since 2012. Nobody's tweeting about housing this week because chips are more exciting, but the builder data just quietly matched a 14-year record for badness.
items.tweetB: 37% of builders cut prices in July, up from 35% in June and 32% in May. That's not noise, that's a monotonic deterioration series running in plain sight while the AI-trade drama eats all the attention.

charts.desc: Deeper charts promised inside the full post — worth opening.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom
items.title: China is sitting on RMB6.8 trillion of unused stimulus room it simply hasn't deployed
items.idea: Ahead of July's Politburo meeting, China still has roughly RMB6.8 trillion of this year's approved government bond issuance quota unused at end-June, plus an RMB800 billion quasi-policy financing instrument and an estimated RMB1.8 trillion of unused bond quota carried over from prior years. H1 2026 GDP grew 4.7%, within reach of the 4.5-5.0% full-year target, so a large immediate stimulus package looks unlikely — expect stronger easing language, not a bazooka.
items.angle: This isn't a capacity problem, it's a sequencing choice — a materially different read than the market's default China-is-out-of-room framing, relevant to anything with China-linked commodity or equity exposure.
items.tweetA: China has RMB6.8 trillion of approved-but-unused bond quota sitting on the shelf, plus another RMB1.8T carried over from prior years. This was never a capacity problem. It's a sequencing choice, and that changes how you should read every China-is-out-of-stimulus-room take.
items.tweetB: H1 China GDP came in at 4.7% against a 4.5-5.0% full-year target — they're basically on track. That's exactly why Beijing doesn't need to fire the big gun at the July Politburo, and why anyone expecting a bazooka this month will be disappointed.

charts.desc: Text-based macro note, no chart images.

items.title: Read through a stablecoin lens, the PayPal bid is vertical integration, not diversification
items.idea: Stripe has spent the past few years quietly assembling almost every layer of a stablecoin empire, including its own Tempo L1. Its $53.4B bid for PayPal (with Advent International) would hand it PayPal's existing payment distribution to route through that rail.
items.angle: Same $53.4B deal as the TheStreet item above, but the stablecoin-specific framing explains the aggressive $60.50/share term in a way the generic fintech-takeover framing doesn't — worth keeping both angles.
items.tweetA: TheStreet calls it a fintech takeover. Bankless calls it a stablecoin land-grab. Both are right, but only one explains why Stripe, not a traditional PE buyer, is at the table with a term this aggressive.
items.tweetB: Stripe already built the stablecoin rail with Tempo. Buying PayPal buys the distribution to actually route volume through it. This is vertical integration, not diversification.

charts.desc: No chart — primer/analysis piece.

items.title: Visa is building its own yield-sharing stablecoin instead of just supporting Circle and Tether
items.idea: Visa's new Visa Stablecoin Platform will let banks and fintechs mint, hold, and move digital dollars, starting with OUSD, a yield-sharing consortium coin launching later this year.
items.angle: A structural threat to the USDT/USDC duopoly: the incumbent card network is choosing to own the rail rather than rent it, which is a different competitive dynamic than the market has been pricing into stablecoin issuers.
items.tweetA: Visa isn't just supporting stablecoins anymore, it's building its own yield-sharing consortium coin, OUSD, with banks and fintechs. That's the incumbents deciding to own the rail instead of renting Circle's.
items.tweetB: The stablecoin market has effectively been a two-horse race between USDT and USDC. Visa's OUSD consortium model is the first serious attempt by the card networks to build a third lane they actually control.

charts.desc: No chart — news brief.

items.title: Citadel Securities' third crypto-exchange bet in a row
items.idea: Citadel Securities put $400 million into Crypto.com at a $20 billion valuation — the exchange's first-ever institutional funding round in a decade of existence — following Citadel's earlier $200M Kraken investment and its co-leading of Ripple's $500M round.
items.angle: A pattern, not a one-off: the largest traditional market maker is building simultaneous exposure across multiple competing crypto venues, which reads as a market-structure land grab rather than a directional crypto bet.
items.tweetA: Citadel Securities just did its third crypto exchange/infra bet in a row: $200M into Kraken, co-led Ripple's $500M round, now $400M into Crypto.com at a $20B valuation. That's not one trade, that's a market-structure land grab.
items.tweetB: Crypto.com took a decade to raise its first institutional round, and Citadel Securities is the one writing the check. When the biggest market maker in traditional finance decides it needs exposure to every major venue, pay attention.

charts.desc: No chart — funding brief.

items.title: The cleanest breadth gauge in the market just hit a record high — and almost nobody's watching it
items.idea: The NYSE advance-decline line counting common stocks only (noise instruments stripped out) just printed the highest level in its history. The broader breadth line made a new all-time high ahead of price in early May — the 13th time since 1940, and 12 of the prior 12 such instances were not followed by a negative rest-of-year. The index's recent pullback was roughly 5% and has already mostly retraced.
items.angle: Directly contradicts the leverage-unwind, deleveraging-cycle framing dominating the rest of this digest — a market-structure bull case that needs to be weighed against the JPMorgan/Korea bear case in the same breath.
items.tweetA: The NYSE advance-decline line, common stocks only, just hit an all-time high. Breadth leading price at a high has happened 13 times since 1940, and 12 of those were not followed by a down year. Everyone's watching chip stocks; almost nobody's watching this.
items.tweetB: Two completely incompatible theses landed in the same inbox this week: leveraged AI ETFs have three more months of forced selling, and the cleanest breadth gauge in the market just hit a record high. Both authors have real data. Only one framing survives the next three months.

charts.desc: NYSE common-stock-only advance-decline line chart — a genuinely rare, tweetable breadth chart.
